State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
:~embers of the Commission 
Dote 
: ~arch 28, 1979 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Sarah Cameron 
Sucject:State Board of Forestry - Is the iDdustry a signi::':"cant 
segment of the public generally under 18703? 
I~TRODUCTION 
Virginia Harwood, one of the iDdustry ma~bers of the 
State Board of Forestry, has requested advice from us con-
cerning her participation in amendments to the Forest Practice 
Rules for the Southern Subdistrict, which generally consists 
of the coastal counties from Marin through Santa Cruz. 
She 
has a significant ownership interest in a company which owns 
approxL~ately 1500 acres of timberland in Santa Cruz County 
and which also manages other timberlands in the subdistrict 
under contract. 
The proposed rules would change stocking 
standards and therefore potentially open more timber land 
managa~ent, (i.e., cutting, restocking, etc.) by perillitting 
reentry to some areas which had been previously logged but 
which for various reasons have not met the restocking standards 
currently in effect. 
The proposed rules would also change the 
trea~~ent of debris left on a logging site and would make 
some changes in the waterbreaks which must be contructed on 
logging sites in order to inhibit erosion. 
Determinations of foreseeability and materiality are 
complicated in this case, and the staff has not yet completed 
those determinations, but a conclusion that there would be 
a foreseeable material effect still leaves the question of 
whether the industry should be considered to be a significant 
segment of the public generally. 
In addition, although the 
other timber industry members on the Board are not involved 
in the area affected by the set of rules currently under con-
sideration, the Board will be considering rules for the district 
which includes Humboldt County in the near future, and all 
timber industry members are working in that area. 
A determination 
of ~~e public generally question at this time will greatly 


Ma~bers of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Two 
facilitate our formulation of aav~ce on any future disquali-
fication questions we receive from the Board. 
DISCUSSION 
The Commission's "public generallyll regulation provides 
that an industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant 
segment of the public if the statute creating an of=icial's 
agency contains a specific fi~ding and declaration like the 
one set forth in the regulation. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
l8703(c). 
As the statute creati~g the Forestry Board requires 
that 0= nine ma~ers, three are to be selected =rom the forest 
products industry and one from the range livestock i~dustry, 
to meet this requirement, the Public Resources Code would have 
to contain language to the effect that: 
The Legislature declares that the indi"iduals 
appointed to the State Board of Forestry are intended 
to represent and further the interest of the forest 
products industry and the range livestock industry 
and that such representation and =urtherance will 
ultimately serve the public interest. 
Accordingly, 
the Legislature finds that for purposes of persons 
who hold such office the forest products industry 
and the range livestock industry are tantamount to 
and constitute the public generally within the meaning 
of Section 87103 of the Government Code. 
There is no such specific declaration in the Public Re-
sources Code. 
We must therefore look to SUbsection (d) of 
Regulation 18703 which provides that: 
... After January 1, 1979, in the absence of an express 
finding and declaration of the type described in 
Paragraph (c) of this section, such an industry, 
trade or professiop constitutes a significant segment 
of the public generally only if such a finding and 
declaration is implicit, taking into account the 
language of the statute, ordinance or other provision 
of law creating or authorizing the creation of the 
agency, the nature and purposes of the program, any 
appl~cable legislative history, and any other rele-
vant circumstances. 
If the forest industry is to constitute a significant seg-
ment of the public generally in this case, the Commission must 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Three 
find ~~at it is L~plicit that members of the Board are intended 
to reoresent and further the interests of the two industries 
involved. 
Obviously, this means something more than that the 
statute requires some members to be appointed from the industry_ 
Other~ise, there would be no need for the specific language set 
forth in the suggested declaration in the regulation. 
Although transcripts of the Commission's deliberations 
when considering this regulation in the early months of 1976 
do not shed any light on the meaning of the '.vords "further and 
represent the interest" of the industry, Daniel Lowenstein, 
Chairman of the Commission, wrote a memorandum to the Commission 
to accompany the dra£t which contained this language, and this 
memo does provide some assistance. 
In justifying a definition 
of "public generally" to mean, under certain circumstances, one 
industrJ, trade or profession (a definition which some ma~ers 
of the Commission thought undercut the purpose and effect of 
the Act), Chairman Lowenstein argued that: 
The Political Reform Act, which is concerned with 
the integrity of governmental processes and not with 
the content of governmental programs, does not 
purport to prevent the Legislature from setting up 
programs to benefit particular industries. 
Nor 
does the Act prevent the Legislature from creating 
boards and commissions to administer such progr~~s 
and directing such boards and commissions to carry 
out the legislative purpose to benefit the particu-
lar industry. 
Memorandum on "Distinguishable 
from the public generally," p. 6 
(Deca~ber 29, 1975) 
In the situation where a member of such a board votes on a 
matter which will affect his own industry, Lowenstein realized 
that there would be a conflict between his private interest 
and the interest of the population at large outside the industry, 
but he also noted that in such a case "[the official's] private 
interest is identical to the 'oublic' interest as defined by 
the legislative purposes of the program he is administering." 
Ibid. 
(Emphasis in original). 
Therefore, there is no conflict 
within the purposes of ~~e Act. 
This is not to say that the Legislature means to benefit 
a particular industry every time it requires industry represen-


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Four 
tation on a board or commission. 
Industry ma~bers have an 
expertise that must be utilized in any rational regulation of 
the industry, and even if the legislative intent is not to 
benefit the industry but to control it for the greater public 
good, limited industry representation on the regulatory body 
itself is a relatively easy way to establish a mechanism for 
providing such expertise. 
On the other hand, there are boards and commissions which 
clearly are created to benefit a particular industry. 
The 
agencies which leap most quickly to mind are various agri-
cultural boards.l/ 
The Legislature's intent to serve the public 
solely by promoting the industry is evident, for example, in 
~~e statute creating the Beef Council. 
The following sections 
are from the Food and Agriculture Code: 
Section 64531. 
Legislative determination. 
It is hereby declared, as a matter of legis-
lative determination, that the provisions of this 
chapter are enacted in the exercise of the power 
of this state for the purposes of protectiDg.and __ 
furthering the public health and welfare. 
It is 
1/ For most of these agencies, the Legislature has already made 
the specific findings and declaration referred to in Regulation 
18703. 
These declarations are contained in the following sections 
of ~~e Food and Agriculture Code: Sec. 901.5, State Board of 
Food and Agriculture; Sec. 6006.5, Cotton Pest Control Board; 
Sec. 6039.5, Curly Top Virus Control Board; Sec. 12041.5, Agri-
cultural Pest Control Advisory Committee; Sec. 14971.5, Feed In-
spection Advisory Committee; Sec. 20462.5, Livestock Identification 
Advisory Board; Sec. 24007.1, an unnamed horseracing advisory 
committee; Sec. 27571.1, Shell Egg Advisory Committee; Sec. 29322.1, 
Apiary Board; Sec. 40571.1, Shipping Point Advisory Committee; 
Sec. 41002, Canning Tomato Advisory Committee; Sec. 41201.1, 
Grape Inspection Advisory Committee; Sec. 52291.1, Seed Advisory 
Committee; Sec. 58852, all advisory boards created oursuant to 
marketing orders; Sec. 64118, Dairy Council; Sec. 64605, Beef 
Council. 
All of these declarations were added to the Code by 
Ch. 1429 of Stats. 1976. 
Interestingly, two boards created since 
then, the Iceburg Lettuce Commission and the Avocado Commission, 
were established by statutes which did not contain such a declara-
tion. 
As the ~declarations and purposes" language used in estab-
lishing these boards is similar to that used with respect to other 
of these agencies, the ommission of the specific declaration is 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Five 
further declared that the cattle industry of this 
state is affected with a public interest, in that, 
among other things: 
(a) 
The production, processing, manufacture, 
and distribution of beef and beef products consti-
tute a paramount industry of this state which not 
only provides substantial and required revenues for 
the state and its political subdivisions, and em-
ployment and a means of livelihood for many thou-
sands of its population, but also furnishes essen-
tial foods that are vital to the public health and 
welfare. 
(b) 
The stabilization, maintenance, and ex-
pansion of nhe cattle industry of California, and 
of the state, nationwide, and foreign markets for 
its products are necessary to assure the consuming 
public an adequate supply of foods which are indispen-
sable in a proper human diet, to protect, for the 
state and its political subdivisions, a necessary 
source of tax revenue, to provide and maintain an 
adequate standard of living for a great segment of 
the population of this state, to maintain proper 
wage scales for those engaged in the cattle industry, 
and to maintain existing employment. 
Section 64532. 
Purposes of Chapter. 
The purposes of this chapter are as follows: 
(a) 
To enable the cattle industry, with the aid 
of the state, to develop, maintain, and to expand the 
state, nationwide, and foreign markets for beef and 
beef products produced, processed, or manufactured 
in this state, and the use and consumption of such 
beef and beef products in such markets. 
(b) 
In aid, but not in limitation, of the pur-
pose in subdivision (a), to authorize and enable the 
director to formulate and effectuate, directly or 
in cooperation with other agencies and instrumen-
talities which are specified in this chapter, sales 
11 (continued) 
probably not significant and is most likely 
due to oversight on the part of the Depar~~ent of Food and 
Agriculture or the Legislature or both. 


Ma~bers of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Six 
stimulation and consumer, or other, educational 
programs designed to increase the use and cons~~p­
tion of beef and beef products. 
Those sections of the Public Resources Code which estab-
lish the State Board of Forestry contain no findings and declar-
ations of legislative intent at all. 
However, the statute 
creating the specific program being administered by the Board 
in the instant situation, the Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice 
Act of 1973, does contain language which identifies the public 
interest being served by the program and which therefore should 
be compared to the language the Legislature used in the Beef 
Council law. 
The legislative intent underlying the Forest 
Practice Act is addressed in the following sections from the 
Public Resources Code: 
Section 4512. 
Findings and declarations. 
(a) 
The Legislature hereby finds and declares 
that the forest resources and timberlands of the 
state are among the most valuable of the natural 
resources of the state and that there is a great 
concern throughout the sta~e relating to their 
utilization, restoration, and protection. 
(b) 
The Legislature further finds and declares 
that the forest resources and timberlands of the 
state furnish high-quality timber, recreational 
opportunities, and aesthetic enjoyment while providing 
watershed protection and maintaining fisheries and 
wildlife. 
(c) 
The Legislature thus declares that it is the 
policy of this state to encourage prudent and res-
ponsible forest resource management calculated to 
serve the public's need for timber and other forest 
products, while giving consideration to the public's 
need for watershed protection, fisheries and wildlife, 
and recreational opportunities alike in this and 
future generations. 
* * * 
Section 4513. 
Intent of Legislature. 
It is the intent of the Legislature to create 
and maintain an effective and comprehensive systa~ 
of regulation and use of all timberlands so as to 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Seven 
assure that: 
(a) 
Where feasible, the productivity of timber-
land is restored, enhanced, and maintained. 
(b) 
The goal of maximum sustained production of 
high-quality timber products is achieved while giving 
consideration to values relating to recreation, 
watershed, wildlife, range and forage, fisheries, 
and aesthetic enjoyment. 
* * * 
The statutory language which requi=es industry representa-
tion on the Forestry Board also differs in significant ways 
from the comparable language with respect to the agricultural 
boards. 
Section 731 of the Public Resources Code, which des-
cribes the qualifications of members of the Forestry Board, 
reads as follows: 
All members of the board shall be appointed 
and shall be selected and approved for appoint-
ment on the basis of their··educational and pro
e
-
fessional qualifications and thei= general 
knowledge of, interest in, and experience with, 
probla~s relating to watershed mangaement (including 
hydrolo~J and soil science), forest managa~ent 
practices, fish and wildlife, range management, 
forest economics, or land use planning. 
Five 
members shall be selected from the general public, 
three members shall be selected from the forest 
products industry, and one member shall be selected 
from the range livestock industry. 
At no time 
shall a majority of the members, nor any of the 
members selected from the general public, be 
persons with a direct personal financial interest, 
within the meaning of Section 1120 of the Govern-
ment Code, in timberlands. 
All members of the board 
shall represent the general public interest. 
(Emphasis added.) 
In contrast, the language with respect to fourteen of the 
seventeen statutorily created (as opposed to creation by 
market order) agricultural boards indicates that the public 
member is appointed to represent the public interest. 
By 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Eight 
implication, ~~e industry me~bers are appoi~ted to represent 
the industry's interest. 
In addition, on eleven of these 
seventeen boards there may not even necessarily be a public 
me~er, as the discretion to appoint such a member lies with 
the Director of the Department of Food and Agriculture.~/ The 
three boards which do not have specified in their statutes that 
the public member represents the public interest, the Seed 
Advisory Board, the Avocado Commission, and the State Board of 
Food and Agriculture, nevertheless also do not have language 
comparable to that in the Forest Practice Act which provides 
that all members represent the public interest; the statutes 
are silent on the question. 
All three, however, have industry 
dominance in terms of the number of me~bers on the Board - there -
are five industry and two public members on the Seed Advisory 
Board; there are fourteen industry members and one public member 
on the Avocado Commission; and there are nine industry, two 
academic and four public members on ~~e State Board of Food and 
Agriculture. 
Lastly, an examination of the history of ~~e Forestry Board 
and the Forest Practice Act is ,relevant in determining whether 
it ·is implicit that the Forestry Board was created to represent 
and further ~~e interests of the forest industry. 
Before 1973, 
the Board consisted of seven members, one of whom was to be a 
public member and each of the other six of whom represented six 
specific fields: 
the pine producing industry, the redwood 
producing industry, forest land ownership, the range livestock 
industry, agriculture, and the beneficial use of water. 
Former 
Pub. Resources Code Sec. 631.11 
Forest practice rules were to 
be formulated by forest practice committees in each of four regional 
districts in the state. 
Prior to 1970, these forest practice 
committees consisted of five members, four of whom represented 
either timber owners or timber operators and one of whom repre-
sented the State Board of Forestry and had no power to vote 
exoept to break a tie. 
Former Pub. Resources Code Sec. 4562-4564. 
Finally, after the forest practice committee formulated proposed 
rules but before they submitted them to the State Board, the 
rules were submitted to the private timber owners within the 
district and two-thirds of those voting had to approve the proposed 
~/ Applicable to all boards noted in n.l, supra., except the 
State Board of Food and Agriculture, the Seed Advisory Board, 
the Cotton Pest Control Board, the Beef Council, the Iceburg 
Lettuce Commission and the Avocado Commission. 
11 
In 1976, the State Board was reoonstituted with much reduced 
industry participation, shifting the majority of the Board members 
to public members. 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Nine 
rules before they were submitted to the State Board. 
Former 
Pub. Resources Code Sec. 4571-4572. 
This scheme was found to be an unconstitutional dele-
gation of legislative authority to ~~e private sector in 
Bayside Timber Co. v. Board of Supervisots of San Mateo County, 
20 C.A. 3d 1 (1971). 
Tae constitutional infirmity seems to 
have consisted of a combination of two shortcomings, that 
"[tJhe Legislature has delegated to timber owners and opera-
tors the exclusive power to formulate forest practice rules 
which, when adopted, have the force and effect of law" and 
that "in this delegation of legislative authority no guides 
or standards to prevent its abuse are laid down by ~~e Act." 
Ibid. at o. 10. 
Thus the reconstitution of the forest oractice 
committee~ in 1970 
(betwee~ the origination of the suit-and 
the court's decision on appeal) to add two more public members 
did not affect the outcome of the case. 
Because of ~~e veto 
power which could still be exercised by one-third of the timber 
owners in the district, the Act ra~ained defective. 
As the 
court concluded, "The Act purports to continue in effect, still 
without standards and with its forest practice rules effectively 
promulgated only by interested members of the timber industry ... 
The constitutional offense is against the public generally of

a

~_., a~ p. 
. 
The Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice Act of 1973 replaced the 
forest practice committee with district technical advisory 
committee. 
Instead of having four industry and three public 
members, these new advisory committees consist of fi~,e public 
members, 
~~ee members from ~~e forest products industry and 
one member from the range livestock industry (their composition 
therefore mimics that of the State Board) all of whom, even 
the industry members, are to represent the general public. 
Pub. Resources Code Sec. 4534. 
The Z'berg-Nejedly Act 
also vested all :authority to adopt forest practice rules with 
the State Board, eliminating both the requirement that the dis-
trict committees first act on them (thereby making the district 
committees advisory) and the requirement for prior approval 
by district timber owners. 
See Pub. Resources Code Sec. 4551, 
~~. 
Finally, the Z'berg-Nejedly Act contained a conflict of 
interest provision which prohibits the members of the State 
Board from participating in any action on enforcement or con-
version of timberlands to other uses "which involves himself 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Ten 
or any person with which he is connected as a director, officer, 
or employee, or in which he has a direct personal financial 
interest within ~~e meaning of Section 1120 of the Government 
Code." 
Pub. Resources Code Sec. 737. 
Govt. Code_ Sec. 1120, 
which was repealed in 1973 as a result of the passage of the 
Moscone Governmental Conflict of Interests and Disclosure Act, 
did not specify when an o.ffical had a personal financial in-
terest, but it did specify when an official did not have such 
an interest. 
With respect to private financial interests, the 
only specified exception was if the financial interest was 
"the ownership of less than 3% of the shares of a corporation for 
profit provided ~~e direct annual income to him from di~lidends, 
including the value of stock dividends, from the corporation 
does not exceed 5% of his total annual income." 
Former Govt. 
Code Sec. 1120(b) (1). 
My research uncovered no cases or 
Attorney General opinions interpreting the phrase "direct 
personal financial interest" as used in Section 1120, but·by 
implication it would have included both the ownership of more 
~~an 3% of the shares of a corporation and the ownership of 
any amount of shares of the corporation when income from those 
shaxes exceeded 5% of the official's annual income. 
CONCLUSION 
wnen all these factors -
the heavily public constitution 
of ~~e Board, 
~~e conflict of interest provisions applicable 
to it, the purposes of the Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice Act, 
and the radical differences between those purposes and the 
ones the Legislature cited in creating the various agricul-
tural boards - are considered, it is difficult to conclude that 
Legislature was implicitly creating the Forest=y Board in order 
to represent and fur~~er the interests of the forest products 
industry or the range livestock industry. 
To the contrary, the 
logical implication is ~~at the Legislature had some concern with 
maintaining a high yield of forest products, but that it wanted 
such a concern to be reconciled with various other interests 
which it found to be important, specifically watershed protec-
tion, fisheries and wildlife and recreational opportunities. 
In this respect, the Legislature's intent cannot be said to 
be more favorable to the forest products industry than it would 
be to almost any other industry it sets out to regulate, as 
most schemes of regulation seek not to destroy or even to unduly 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Elegen 
impair an industry but instead to encourage its development to 
the extent possible consistent with other public purooses. 
·In the instant case, as in other such situations, the in-
terests of the regulated industry are clearly not, in the Legis-
lature's mind, synonymous with the int~rests of the public. 
The 
public purpose of the agriculture boards is solely to en-
courage ~,e development of ~,e industry; with respect to the 
ForestrJ Board, the public's interest is stated to be not only 
to develop the forest products industry or the range live-
stock industry but also to protect watershed, fisheries and 
wildlife and recreational opportunities. 
The Forest Practice 
Act, unlike the statutes creating the various agriculture 
boards, cannot ~,erefore be said to recognize a congruence 
between the industry's interest and the public interest. 
For 
this reason, I recommend that the forest products industry and 
~,e range livestock industry should not be found to be a signi-
ficant segment of ~~e public with respect to decisions made by 
members of ~,e State Board of Forestry. 
STC:nrw

sections.question: Is the forest products industry a significant segment of the public generally under Section 18703 of the Government Code, particularly regarding Virginia Harwood's participation in State Board of Forestry decisions on Forest Practice Rules amendments?
sections.conclusion:
wnen all these factors -
the heavily public constitution 
of ~~e Board, 
~~e conflict of interest provisions applicable 
to it, the purposes of the Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice Act, 
and the radical differences between those purposes and the 
ones the Legislature cited in creating the various agricul-
tural boards - are considered, it is difficult to conclude that 
Legislature was implicitly creating the Forest=y Board in order 
to represent and fur~~er the interests of the forest products 
industry or the range livestock industry. 
To the contrary, the 
logical implication is ~~at the Legislature had some concern with 
maintaining a high yield of forest products, but that it wanted 
such a concern to be reconciled with various other interests 
which it found to be important, specifically watershed protec-
tion, fisheries and wildlife and recreational opportunities. 
In this respect, the Legislature's intent cannot be said to 
be more favorable to the forest products industry than it would 
be to almost any other industry it sets out to regulate, as 
most schemes of regulation seek not to destroy or even to unduly 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Elegen 
impair an industry but instead to encourage its development to 
the extent possible consistent with other public purooses. 
·In the instant case, as in other such situations, the in-
terests of the regulated industry are clearly not, in the Legis-
lature's mind, synonymous with the int~rests of the public. 
The 
public purpose of the agriculture boards is solely to en-
courage ~,e development of ~,e industry; with respect to the 
ForestrJ Board, the public's interest is stated to be not only 
to develop the forest products industry or the range live-
stock industry but also to protect watershed, fisheries and 
wildlife and recreational opportunities. 
The Forest Practice 
Act, unlike the statutes creating the various agriculture 
boards, cannot ~,erefore be said to recognize a congruence 
between the industry's interest and the public interest. 
For 
this reason, I recommend that the forest products industry and 
~,e range livestock industry should not be found to be a signi-
ficant segment of ~~e public with respect to decisions made by 
members of ~,e State Board of Forestry.

one set forth in the regulation.

l8703(c).

products industry and the range livestock industry 
and that such representation and =urtherance will 
ultimately serve the public interest. 
Accordingly, 
the Legislature finds that for purposes of persons 
who hold such office the forest products industry 
and the range livestock industry are tantamount to 
and constitute the public generally within the meaning 
of Section 87103 of the Government Code.

sources Code.

... After January 1, 1979, in the absence of an express 
finding and declaration of the type described in 
Paragraph (c) of this section, such an industry, 
trade or professiop constitutes a significant segment 
of the public generally only if such a finding and 
declaration is implicit, taking into account the 
language of the statute, ordinance or other provision 
of law creating or authorizing the creation of the 
agency, the nature and purposes of the program, any 
appl~cable legislative history, and any other rele-
vant circumstances.

ment of the public generally in this case, the Commission must

purport to prevent the Legislature from setting up

Nor 
does the Act prevent the Legislature from creating 
boards and commissions to administer such progr~~s 
and directing such boards and commissions to carry 
out the legislative purpose to benefit the particu-
lar industry.

from the public generally," p. 6 
(Deca~ber 29, 1975) 
In the situation where a member of such a board votes on a

but he also noted that in such a case "[the official's] private

Ibid. 
(Emphasis in original).

within the purposes of ~~e Act.

a particular industry every time it requires industry represen-

tation on a board or commission.

expertise that must be utilized in any rational regulation of 
the industry, and even if the legislative intent is not to

good, limited industry representation on the regulatory body 
itself is a relatively easy way to establish a mechanism for

marketing orders; Sec. 64118, Dairy Council; Sec. 64605, Beef 
Council. 
All of these declarations were added to the Code by 
Ch. 1429 of Stats. 1976. 
Interestingly, two boards created since 
then, the Iceburg Lettuce Commission and the Avocado Commission, 
were established by statutes which did not contain such a declara-
tion. 
As the ~declarations and purposes" language used in estab-
lishing these boards is similar to that used with respect to other 
of these agencies, the ommission of the specific declaration is 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Five 
further declared that the cattle industry of this 
state is affected with a public interest, in that, 
among other things: 
(a) 
The production, processing, manufacture, 
and distribution of beef and beef products consti-
tute a paramount industry of this state which not 
only provides substantial and required revenues for 
the state and its political subdivisions, and em-
ployment and a means of livelihood for many thou-
sands of its population, but also furnishes essen-
tial foods that are vital to the public health and 
welfare. 
(b) 
The stabilization, maintenance, and ex-
pansion of nhe cattle industry of California, and 
of the state, nationwide, and foreign markets for 
its products are necessary to assure the consuming 
public an adequate supply of foods which are indispen-
sable in a proper human diet, to protect, for the 
state and its political subdivisions, a necessary 
source of tax revenue, to provide and maintain an 
adequate standard of living for a great segment of 
the population of this state, to maintain proper 
wage scales for those engaged in the cattle industry, 
and to maintain existing employment. 
Section 64532. 
Purposes of Chapter. 
The purposes of this chapter are as follows: 
(a) 
To enable the cattle industry, with the aid 
of the state, to develop, maintain, and to expand the 
state, nationwide, and foreign markets for beef and 
beef products produced, processed, or manufactured 
in this state, and the use and consumption of such 
beef and beef products in such markets. 
(b) 
In aid, but not in limitation, of the pur-
pose in subdivision (a), to authorize and enable the 
director to formulate and effectuate, directly or 
in cooperation with other agencies and instrumen-
talities which are specified in this chapter, sales 
11 (continued) 
probably not significant and is most likely 
due to oversight on the part of the Depar~~ent of Food and 
Agriculture or the Legislature or both. 


Ma~bers of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Six 
stimulation and consumer, or other, educational 
programs designed to increase the use and cons~~p­
tion of beef and beef products. 
Those sections of the Public Resources Code which estab-
lish the State Board of Forestry contain no findings and declar-
ations of legislative intent at all. 
However, the statute 
creating the specific program being administered by the Board 
in the instant situation, the Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice 
Act of 1973, does contain language which identifies the public 
interest being served by the program and which therefore should 
be compared to the language the Legislature used in the Beef 
Council law. 
The legislative intent underlying the Forest 
Practice Act is addressed in the following sections from the 
Public Resources Code: 
Section 4512. 
Findings and declarations. 
(a) 
The Legislature hereby finds and declares 
that the forest resources and timberlands of the 
state are among the most valuable of the natural 
resources of the state and that there is a great 
concern throughout the sta~e relating to their 
utilization, restoration, and protection. 
(b) 
The Legislature further finds and declares 
that the forest resources and timberlands of the 
state furnish high-quality timber, recreational 
opportunities, and aesthetic enjoyment while providing 
watershed protection and maintaining fisheries and 
wildlife. 
(c) 
The Legislature thus declares that it is the 
policy of this state to encourage prudent and res-
ponsible forest resource management calculated to 
serve the public's need for timber and other forest 
products, while giving consideration to the public's 
need for watershed protection, fisheries and wildlife, 
and recreational opportunities alike in this and 
future generations.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the forest products industry constitutes a significant segment of the public generally under FPPC regulations when determining if a State Board of Forestry industry member must disqualify from voting on forest practice rule amendments affecting her company's timber interests.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The document analyzes the legal framework for determining whether an industry constitutes a significant segment of the public generally, examining both explicit statutory declarations and implicit legislative intent through the Forest Practice Act's findings and the Beef Council statute as comparative examples.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Internal memorandum to Commission members rather than formal advice letter; no discrete CONCLUSION section with final determination; document appears truncated at end; mixed OCR quality with some scanning artifacts ('iDdustry', 'ma~bers'); discussion section provides substantive legal analysis comparing implicit vs. explicit legislative findings

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Is the forest products industry a significant segment of the public generally under Section 18703 of the Government Code, particularly regarding Virginia Harwood's participation in State Board of Forestry decisions on Forest Practice Rules amendments?

CONCLUSION: wnen all these factors -
the heavily public constitution 
of ~~e Board, 
~~e conflict of interest provisions applicable 
to it, the purposes of the Z'berg-Nejedly Forest Practice Act, 
and the radical differences between those purposes and the 
ones the Legislature cited in creating the various agricul-
tural boards - are considered, it is difficult to conclude that 
Legislature was implicitly creating the Forest=y Board in order 
to represent and fur~~er the interests of the forest products 
industry or the range livestock industry. 
To the contrary, the 
logical implication is ~~at the Legislature had some concern with 
maintaining a high yield of forest products, but that it wanted 
such a concern to be reconciled with various other interests 
which it found to be important, specifically watershed protec-
tion, fisheries and wildlife and recreational opportunities. 
In this respect, the Legislature's intent cannot be said to 
be more favorable to the forest products industry than it would 
be to almost any other industry it sets out to regulate, as 
most schemes of regulation seek not to destroy or even to unduly 


Members of the Commission 
March 28, 1979 
Page Elegen 
impair an industry but instead to encourage its development to 
the extent possible consistent with other public purooses. 
·In the instant case, as in other such situations, the in-
terests of the regulated industry are clearly not, in the Legis-
lature's mind, synonymous with the int~rests of the public. 
The 
public purpose of the agriculture boards is solely to en-
courage ~,e development of ~,e industry; with respect to the 
ForestrJ Board, the public's interest is stated to be not only 
to develop the forest products industry or the range live-
stock industry but also to protect watershed, fisheries and 
wildlife and recreational opportunities. 
The Forest Practice 
Act, unlike the statutes creating the various agriculture 
boards, cannot ~,erefore be said to recognize a congruence 
between the industry's interest and the public interest. 
For 
this reason, I recommend that the forest products industry and 
~,e range livestock industry should not be found to be a signi-
ficant segment of ~~e public with respect to decisions made by 
members of ~,e State Board of Forestry. 
STC:nrw
embedding.first_500_words: State of California Memorandum To :~embers of the Commission Dote : ~arch 28, 1979 From FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Sarah Cameron Sucject:State Board of Forestry - Is the iDdustry a signi::':"cant segment of the public generally under 18703? I~TRODUCTION Virginia Harwood, one of the iDdustry ma~bers of the State Board of Forestry, has requested advice from us con- cerning her participation in amendments to the Forest Practice Rules for the Southern Subdistrict, which generally consists of the coastal counties from Marin through Santa Cruz. She has a significant ownership interest in a company which owns approxL~ately 1500 acres of timberland in Santa Cruz County and which also manages other timberlands in the subdistrict under contract. The proposed rules would change stocking standards and therefore potentially open more timber land managa~ent, (i.e., cutting, restocking, etc.) by perillitting reentry to some areas which had been previously logged but which for various reasons have not met the restocking standards currently in effect. The proposed rules would also change the trea~~ent of debris left on a logging site and would make some changes in the waterbreaks which must be contructed on logging sites in order to inhibit erosion. Determinations of foreseeability and materiality are complicated in this case, and the staff has not yet completed those determinations, but a conclusion that there would be a foreseeable material effect still leaves the question of whether the industry should be considered to be a significant segment of the public generally. In addition, although the other timber industry members on the Board are not involved in the area affected by the set of rules currently under con- sideration, the Board will be considering rules for the district which includes Humboldt County in the near future, and all timber industry members are working in that area. A determination of ~~e public generally question at this time will greatly Ma~bers of the Commission March 28, 1979 Page Two facilitate our formulation of aav~ce on any future disquali- fication questions we receive from the Board. DISCUSSION The Commission's "public generallyll regulation provides that an industry, trade or profession constitutes a significant segment of the public if the statute creating an of=icial's agency contains a specific fi~ding and declaration like the one set forth in the regulation. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section l8703(c). As the statute creati~g the Forestry Board requires that 0= nine ma~ers, three are to be selected =rom the forest products industry and one from the range livestock i~dustry, to meet this requirement, the Public Resources Code would have to contain language to the effect that: The Legislature declares that the indi"iduals appointed to the State Board of Forestry are intended to represent and further the interest of the forest products industry and the range livestock industry and that such representation and =urtherance will ultimately serve the public interest. Accordingly, the Legislature finds that for purposes of persons who hold such office the forest products industry and the range livestock industry are tantamount to and constitute the public
embedding.summary: This internal FPPC memorandum analyzes whether the forest products industry qualifies as a significant segment of the public generally under Regulation 18703 when determining disqualification requirements for an industry member of the State Board of Forestry voting on forest practice rule amendments. The analysis examines statutory language, legislative intent, and comparative industry board structures.
